text: Portsmouth colorectal presents lymphatic drainage of the splenic flexure defined by intraoperative ICG mapping. In this video, we aim to analyze the steps and technical details of the intraoperative use of ICG for lymph node mapping during the surgical treatment of a splenic flexure tumor. The lymphatic drainage of the splenic flexure remains purely defined and it is often assumed to be heterogeneous, contributing to surgical uncertainty.

It is usually directed to the left colic pedicle, but occasionally to the left middle colic. This is the case of a sixty-three-year-old gentleman who underwent a robotic colonic resection for a splenic flexure tumor, T-III-N-I-M-Zero.

The surgeon performs a peritumoral injection of Indocyanine Green (ICG) into the subsorosal layer, with the fluorescence passing to the drain and leaf nodes within fifteen minutes, visualized under near-infrared light.

The surgeon identifies the lymphatic drainage patterns of the tumor, revealing left colic drainage. A left hemicolectomy is performed, ligating both the left colic and the left branch of the middle colic artery, which is appropriate for oncological treatment.

The surgeon employs an infracolic three-step approach to mobilize the splenic flexure using a single jockeying technique. Special camera filters are utilized to visualize the ICG fluorescence, allowing the surgeon to switch between normal and fluorescence viewing modes in high definition.

The surgeon performs a high ligation of the inferior mesenteric vein (IMV) using robotic hemologs. The pancreas is identified, and its tail is carefully separated from the splenic flexure of the colon.

The surgeon divides the mesentery up to the level of the small bowel and performs colonic transection. A volume of indocyanine green (ICG) is injected to visualize and check the blood supply at both ends of the bowel at the transection site using fluorescence imaging.

The surgeon performs an intracorporeal anastomosis using robotic assistance, closing the enterotomy with a continuous suture. A second layer of sutures is applied in a continuous running fashion to reinforce the anastomosis.
There were no postoperative complications and the patient was discharged on fourth postoperative day. The pathology report revealed twenty-one lymph nodes harvested, PT three and one.

This case demonstrates that ICG is useful for identifying lymph node drainage in case of splenic flexure tumours where the pattern is variable. A real-time visualisation of lymphatic mapping allows the surgeon to determine the type of resection needed.
